title: Photosynthesis — how plants make food from light
examTip: AQA tests the word equation, the symbol equation, the location of photosynthesis, and the two stages. You need to know both the light-dependent and light-independent (Calvin cycle) stages exist, what goes in and comes out of each, and be able to balance the symbol equation.
introTips: Photosynthesis is the foundation of almost all food chains — get this right and ecology becomes much clearer.
You need both the word equation and the symbol equation — practise writing the symbol equation from memory.
The two-stage structure (light-dependent and light-independent) is the most demanding part of this topic for GCSE.
intro: Every time you eat, you're ultimately powered by sunlight. Plants — and algae — capture light energy and use it to build sugar from air and water. That sugar is the starting point of almost every food chain on Earth. Photosynthesis is one of the most important chemical reactions that has ever evolved, and understanding it tells you where your energy comes from.

keyPoints.heading: The word equation — and why it matters
keyPoints.content: The word equation for photosynthesis is: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen. Light energy is required for this to happen, and it is absorbed by the green pigment chlorophyll. Because plants make their own food using light energy, they are called autotrophs (or producers). This is what makes plants fundamentally different from animals — you and every other animal are heterotrophs: you must eat to get energy. Glucose produced by photosynthesis has two uses: it can be used immediately in respiration (to release energy for cell processes), or it can be converted and stored — as starch for storage, cellulose for cell walls, or proteins and lipids for growth.
writeBullets: Word equation: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen
Light energy required — absorbed by chlorophyll
Plants are autotrophs: they make their own food using light
Animals are heterotrophs: must eat to get energy
Glucose uses: respiration (energy), starch (storage), cellulose (cell walls), proteins and lipids (growth)

keyTerms.def: The process by which plants (and algae) use light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen
An organism that makes its own food using an external energy source such as light — plants and algae are autotrophs
An organism that must consume other organisms to obtain energy — animals are heterotrophs
The green pigment found in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy (mainly red and blue wavelengths) for photosynthesis
The sugar produced by photosynthesis — used in respiration for energy or converted to other molecules for storage and growth

keyPoints.memTip: The word equation has four chemicals: two in, two out. CO2 and water go IN; glucose and oxygen come OUT. Light energy is the input that drives it. Write the equation from memory every day until it's instant. Then ask yourself: where does each product go? Glucose → respiration or storage. Oxygen → released into the air (handy for us).

keyPoints.heading: The symbol equation
keyPoints.content: The balanced symbol equation for photosynthesis is: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Read it as: six molecules of carbon dioxide plus six molecules of water produce one molecule of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) plus six molecules of oxygen. Check the balance: on the left, you have 6 carbons, 18 oxygens, 12 hydrogens. On the right: glucose has 6 carbons, 6 oxygens, 12 hydrogens — and 6O₂ gives another 12 oxygens. That's 6 carbons, 18 oxygens, 12 hydrogens on both sides. Balanced. The number 6 appears in front of three of the four molecules — the only exception is glucose, which is written without a coefficient because one molecule already contains all six carbons.
writeBullets: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂
6 molecules of CO₂ + 6 molecules of water → 1 molecule of glucose + 6 molecules of O₂
Balanced: 6C, 18O, 12H on each side
Glucose formula: C₆H₁₂O₆ (6 carbons, 12 hydrogens, 6 oxygens)
Coefficient of 6 applies to CO₂, H₂O, and O₂ — glucose has no coefficient (it is 1)

keyTerms.def: The chemical formula for glucose — 6 carbon atoms, 12 hydrogen atoms, 6 oxygen atoms
A chemical equation where the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides — matter is conserved

keyPoints.memTip: Six-six-one-six: 6CO₂, 6H₂O → 1C₆H₁₂O₆, 6O₂. Say it rhythmically until it's automatic. The only oddity is glucose — memorise C₆H₁₂O₆ as '6-12-6'. Then practise checking the balance on each side. Examiners WILL ask you to write this equation — there are usually two free marks just for getting it right.

keyPoints.heading: Where photosynthesis happens — chloroplasts and leaf structure
keyPoints.content: Photosynthesis takes place inside chloroplasts, which contain the pigment chlorophyll. Chlorophyll absorbs light — mainly red and blue wavelengths. It reflects green light, which is why plants appear green to your eyes. Within the leaf, the cells that carry out the most photosynthesis are the palisade mesophyll cells. These are tall, column-shaped cells packed with chloroplasts, positioned near the top surface of the leaf — closest to the light. The arrangement is a neat example of structure matching function: the cells that need the most light are closest to the light source, and they're densely packed so light passes through as many chloroplasts as possible.
writeBullets: Photosynthesis occurs inside chloroplasts
Chlorophyll absorbs mainly red and blue light — reflects green light
Plants look green because green light is reflected, not absorbed
Palisade mesophyll cells: tall, packed with chloroplasts, near top of leaf
Position at the top of the leaf = closest to light source

keyTerms.def: The organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis takes place — contains chlorophyll and the internal membrane structures for each stage
A tall, chloroplast-rich cell near the top of the leaf that carries out the majority of photosynthesis
A flattened membrane disc inside the chloroplast where the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur
The fluid-filled space inside the chloroplast, surrounding the thylakoids — where the light-independent (Calvin cycle) reactions occur

keyPoints.memTip: Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue but reflects green — that's why every leaf is green. If an exam question asks 'which colours of light does chlorophyll absorb?', the answer is red and blue (not green). And always connect palisade cells to their position — top of the leaf, closest to light, packed with chloroplasts. That's a structure-function link AQA loves.

keyPoints.heading: The two stages of photosynthesis
keyPoints.content: Photosynthesis happens in two connected stages inside the chloroplast. Stage 1 — Light-dependent reactions (in the thylakoid membranes): light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll and used to split water molecules (photolysis). This releases oxygen as a by-product (which diffuses out of the leaf), and the energy from light is used to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Stage 2 — Light-independent reactions / Calvin cycle (in the stroma): carbon dioxide from the air is fixed using the ATP made in Stage 1. Through a cycle of reactions, carbon dioxide and hydrogen (from split water) are combined to build glucose. This stage does not directly require light, but it depends on the ATP produced in Stage 1 — so if light stops, Stage 2 eventually stops too. For AQA GCSE, you need to know that both stages exist, where they happen, and what goes in and comes out of each. You do not need to know every enzyme and intermediate molecule.
writeBullets: Stage 1 — Light-dependent: in thylakoid membranes; light splits water (photolysis); produces ATP and O₂
Oxygen from Stage 1 is released as a by-product
Stage 2 — Light-independent / Calvin cycle: in stroma; uses CO₂ + ATP to make glucose
Stage 2 does not directly need light but depends on ATP from Stage 1
If light stops: Stage 1 stops → no ATP → Stage 2 eventually stops

keyTerms.def: The first stage of photosynthesis, occurring in thylakoid membranes — light splits water, releases O₂, and produces ATP
The second stage of photosynthesis (Calvin cycle), occurring in the stroma — uses CO₂ and ATP from Stage 1 to produce glucose
The cycle of reactions in the stroma that fixes CO₂ into glucose using ATP from the light-dependent reactions
The splitting of water molecules using light energy in the thylakoid membranes — releases oxygen and hydrogen
Adenosine triphosphate — the cell's energy currency, produced in Stage 1 and used to power Stage 2 of photosynthesis

keyPoints.memTip: Two stages, two locations: LIGHT = thylakoid membranes (think of thylakoids as tiny solar panels). DARK (Calvin) = stroma (think of the stroma as the factory floor). Light stage splits water and makes ATP. Dark stage uses CO₂ and ATP to build glucose. If you can remember WHERE and WHAT goes IN and OUT, you can answer any two-stage question.

tables.title: The two stages of photosynthesis — inputs and outputs
rows: ATP, oxygen (O₂) — O₂ released as by-product
Yes — light is the energy source
No — but needs ATP from Stage 1

1.question: Which of the following correctly states the word equation for photosynthesis?
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right — carbon dioxide and water go in, glucose and oxygen come out. Light energy drives the reaction, absorbed by chlorophyll.
1.wrongFeedback: The correct word equation is: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen. Remember: CO₂ and water are the INPUTS (raw materials); glucose and oxygen are the OUTPUTS. Light energy is needed to make it happen.

2.question: Which is the correct balanced symbol equation for photosynthesis?
2.correctFeedback: Perfect — 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. The 6s in front of CO₂, H₂O, and O₂ balance the equation. Glucose is written once because one molecule contains all six carbons.
2.wrongFeedback: The balanced equation is 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. You need 6 molecules of CO₂ and 6 of water to make 1 glucose and 6 O₂. Remember: 6-6 → 1-6. Glucose doesn't get a coefficient of 6 — it IS the six-carbon molecule.

commonMistakes: Reversing the word equation — writing 'glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water' is the equation for respiration, not photosynthesis. They are opposite processes.
Forgetting the coefficients in the symbol equation — writing CO₂ + H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + O₂ is unbalanced. Always include the 6s in front of CO₂, H₂O, and O₂.
Saying chlorophyll absorbs green light — it reflects green light (that's why plants are green). Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light.
Thinking that Stage 2 (Calvin cycle) doesn't need light at all — while it doesn't use light directly, it depends entirely on ATP produced in Stage 1. No light → eventually no glucose.
Saying photosynthesis occurs in the cell membrane — it occurs in chloroplasts, specifically the thylakoid membranes (Stage 1) and the stroma (Stage 2).
examTips: When asked for the symbol equation, write it, then check the balance on both sides: count C, H, and O atoms. One balanced equation is worth two marks — it's worth the 30 seconds to verify.

For a question about why plants appear green, the answer must mention that chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light and reflects green light — 'reflects green' is the key phrase the examiner is looking for.

Two-stage questions often ask 'what is produced in Stage 1 that is used in Stage 2?' — the answer is ATP. Alternatively you may be asked where each stage happens: thylakoid membranes (Stage 1) and stroma (Stage 2).

revisionCardBullets: Word equation: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen (light energy, chlorophyll)
Symbol equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — memorise the 6-6-1-6 pattern
Plants are autotrophs — make their own food using light energy
Chlorophyll absorbs RED and BLUE light — reflects green (why plants look green)
Photosynthesis happens in chloroplasts — palisade mesophyll cells have the most
Palisade cells: tall, packed with chloroplasts, at top of leaf closest to light
Stage 1 (light-dependent): thylakoid membranes — light splits water, makes ATP, releases O₂
Stage 2 (Calvin cycle): stroma — CO₂ + ATP → glucose; does not use light directly
Stage 2 still depends on Stage 1 — no light = no ATP = eventually no glucose
